ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general green bean food product class; no specific entry for salted-egg-coated stir-fried French beans exists. Closest defensible parent class used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers cooked green/string beans as the closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for the salted egg yolk sauce preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish preparation. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for Chinese stir-fried vegetable dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of baby French beans with salted egg is approximately 200 g, typically shared as a side dish at a Chinese meal for 2–4 persons.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Green snap beans, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169961) combined with salted duck egg yolk composition (FDC ID 172185) and butter (FDC ID 173410); proportional blend estimated at approximately 60% French beans, 25% salted egg yolk sauce, 15% butter/oil/evaporated milk
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for baby French beans with salted egg exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by proportional blending of USDA FDC entries for cooked green snap beans, salted duck egg yolk, butter, and evaporated milk. Input record values (energy 190 kcal, protein 5.25 g, fat 16.0 g, carbohydrate 7.5 g, sodium 425 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this blended estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB Foodo was consulted but does not carry a direct entry for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; green beans (string beans, snap beans) GI = 32 (glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baby French beans with salted egg yolk sauce. GI of 32 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 for cooked green/snap beans (glucose reference). The salted egg yolk sauce contributes predominantly fat and protein, which do not raise GI; the overall dish GI is therefore anchored to the bean component. GL per serving calculated as: GI (32) × available carbohydrate per serving (200 g × 7.5 g / 100 g = 15 g) / 100 = 4.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 425 mg per 100 g, driven by salted duck egg yolk; a 200 g serving delivers 850 mg sodium, representing 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g per meal and request reduced salted egg yolk quantity; avoid adding extra salt or soy sauce during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 32) and low available carbohydrate (7.5 g per 100 g) with 3 g dietary fibre per 100 g result in a low glycemic load of 4.8 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; the low GI and high fibre content support glycaemic control, but the high fat content (16 g per 100 g) should be factored into total daily energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 6.0 g per 100 g and cholesterol at 175 mg per 100 g, both driven by salted duck egg yolk and butter, elevate LDL-cholesterol risk; sodium at 425 mg per 100 g further increases cardiovascular burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions (≤100 g), request reduced salted egg yolk and butter, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium or high-saturated-fat dishes in the same meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g per 100 g and sodium 425 mg per 100 g may promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6) and tissue fluid retention.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise consumption of this dish; substituting butter with extra-virgin olive oil and reducing salted egg yolk quantity would lower the pro-inflammatory fat and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baby French Beans with Salted Egg (咸蛋四季豆) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean stir-fried side dish in which tender baby French beans are wok-fried and coated in a rich, creamy, golden salted duck egg yolk sauce. The sauce is typically made by mashing cured salted duck egg yolks and cooking them in butter or oil with curry leaves, chilli padi, and sometimes evaporated milk until the mixture turns foamy and fragrant. The beans are then tossed through the sauce until evenly coated. The dish delivers a contrast of textures — crisp-tender beans against a velvety, umami-rich sauce — and a flavour profile that is simultaneously savoury, slightly spicy, and intensely eggy. It is commonly served as a shared side dish at Chinese zi char restaurants and hawker centres across Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is calorie-dense at approximately 190 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content (16 g per 100 g, of which 6 g is saturated fat) and elevated cholesterol (175 mg per 100 g) attributable to the salted egg yolk and butter. Sodium is high at 425 mg per 100 g, making the dish a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. However, the low glycemic index (GI 32) and moderate dietary fibre (3 g per 100 g) make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions. The French beans contribute vitamin C (7.5 mg per 100 g), iron (0.75 mg per 100 g), and calcium (40 mg per 100 g). The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free but is not halal due to the use of salted duck egg and is not vegan due to egg and dairy components.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for baby French beans with salted egg exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by proportional blending of approved USDA FDC component entries (cooked green snap beans, salted duck egg yolk, butter, evaporated milk). The input record values are internally consistent with this blended estimate and have been retained. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (cooked green/snap beans, Atkinson et al. 2021). Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of a direct approved-source entry.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite stir-fried dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2024; the blended component approach does not yield reliable MUFA/PUFA sub-fractions. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2024. potassium_mg value of 220 mg per 100 g is an estimate derived from the French bean component (USDA FDC cooked green beans ~146 mg per 100 g) adjusted for the full dish composition; this value carries low confidence and is flagged for future verification. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g per 100 g is a trace amount consistent with butter content; this is not a confirmed zero. Halal status is uncertain: salted duck eggs may not be halal-certified depending on supplier; is_halal is set to false pending verification. The dietary_tags 'vegetarian' and 'gluten_free' from the input record have been preserved; 'Dim Sum' ethnic tag from input has been removed as this dish is a zi char side dish, not a dim sum item. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate from cooked green/snap beans (Atkinson et al. 2021 / University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the literature.
